epigraph.text: In my Father's house are many rooms.

content.text:
Quadrilateralsâfour-sided polygonsâare everywhere: windows, doors, 
floors, screens, books, and buildings. Understanding their properties 
helps us work with the shapes we encounter daily.

content.title: Quadrilateral
content.content:
A **quadrilateral** is a closed figure with 
four straight sides and four angles. The sum of the interior angles 
is always $360ÂḞ$.

content.text:
Think of it this way: a quadrilateral can be divided into two triangles, 
and $2 \times 180ÂḞ = 360ÂḞ$.

content.title: Parallelogram
content.content:
A **parallelogram** has two pairs of parallel 
sides. Opposite sides are equal, and opposite angles are equal.

content.title: Rectangle
content.content:
A **rectangle** is a parallelogram with four 
right angles ($90ÂḞ$ each).

content.title: Square
content.content: A **square** is a rectangle with four equal sides.

content.title: Rhombus
content.content:
A **rhombus** is a parallelogram with four equal 
sides (but not necessarily right angles).

content.title: Trapezoid
content.content:
A **trapezoid** has exactly one pair of parallel 
sides (called bases).

content.text: The special quadrilaterals form a hierarchy:

items: Every **square** is also a rectangle, rhombus, and parallelogram
Not every parallelogram is a rectangle or rhombus

content.content:
Just as quadrilaterals form a hierarchy of relationships, the body 
of Christ has an ordered structure. "Now you are the body of Christ, 
and each one of you is a part of it" (1 Corinthians 12:27). Each 
shape has its place and purpose in the geometric world.

content.title: Quadrilateral Angle Sum
content.content: The sum of the interior angles of any quadrilateral is $360ÂḞ$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Finding a missing angle
content.problem:
A quadrilateral has angles measuring $85ÂḞ$, $100ÂḞ$, and $90ÂḞ$. Find 
the fourth angle.

content.title: Algebraic quadrilateral problem
content.problem:
The angles of a quadrilateral measure $x$, $(x + 20)ÂḞ$, $(x + 40)ÂḞ$, 
and $(x + 60)ÂḞ$. Find all four angles.

content.title: Scripture
content.content:
The New Jerusalem is described as "a square, as long as it was wide" 
(Revelation 21:16). The square, with its perfect symmetry, represents 
completeness and divine order. Its 360ÂḞ totalâa full circleâsuggests 
wholeness and eternity.

marginNotes.text:
The city was laid out like a square, 
as long as it was wide.

Build an altar of acacia wood, three 
cubits high; it is to be square, five cubits long and five cubits wide.

And he measured the court, a hundred 
cubits long and a hundred cubits broad, foursquare.

marginNotes.author: Aristotle
marginNotes.text: The whole is greater than the sum of its parts.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
All of humanity's problems stem from man's 
inability to sit quietly in a room alone.
